headline: Licensed vs. Unlicensed Handyman in Nevada: When a Las Vegas “Big Job” Needs a Real License
description: Out here in the valley, “small” issues stack into big scopes fast—doors drifting, cracks reappearing
abstract: Out here in the valley, “small” issues stack into big scopes fast—doors drifting, cracks reappearing, trim opening up—partly because the local ground includes loamy, carbonatic soils with a petrocalcic (caliche) layer
articleSection: Related Guides You Might Find Helpful

mainEntity.name: If we’re targeting high-ticket work, what’s the cleanest rule to remember?

acceptedAnswer.text: Nevada’s Contractors Board describes an exemption for repair/maintenance work **valued at less than $1,000** (labor + materials) with important exceptions—high-ticket scopes typically don’t fit that box.

mainEntity.name: Does “no permit needed” automatically mean “handyman ok”?

acceptedAnswer.text: Not automatically. Permit status matters a lot, but the Board also frames the exemption around **project value and conditions**, so scope and category still matter.

mainEntity.name: What’s the real risk with unlicensed contracting on bigger jobs?

acceptedAnswer.text: Nevada spells out escalating penalties for unlicensed contracting (including repeat-offense escalation).

mainEntity.name: Why do Vegas projects “grow legs” so often?

acceptedAnswer.text: Caliche-layer soils can show up as small alignment drift, and heat cycling beats up caulk/fasteners—so one visible symptom can hide several connected fixes.

mainEntity.name: What should be tight and clear on a high-ticket scope?

acceptedAnswer.text: Exact inclusions/exclusions, material specs, how change-orders are handled, and what happens if hidden damage shows up (water paths, rot behind trim, etc.).
